chunks.text: Living abroad, one sees both the good and the bad of one's own country.
At thirty, I was posted to the United States for three years for work.
The first months brought surprise from cultural differences.
Ordering on behalf of yourself at restaurants, the gesture of a shrug.
" Different " kept appearing.
Americans are direct.
They do not expose to hide emotions.
If they disagree, they argue clearly, as if to bet on it.
On the other hand, there is casualness.
With someone in the same elevator, small talk that is not rude begins immediately.
But I also saw the dark side.
Gun crime, racial discrimination, the high cost of medical care.
Hundreds of millions of people have no health insurance, and if they get sick
they must bind their life to debt.
A homeless person carrying their gear lies at the foot of a skyscraper.
Returning to Japan, I realized anew: safety, cleanliness, the precision of time.
In Japan
you can see a woman almost asleep walking alone at a station late at night.
You would not see this in America.
But Japan has its weaknesses too.
Conformity pressure, overwork, high suicide rates.
" The individual " is weak, and people, like a bishop
pay too much heed to the eyes of others.
The experience of another culture is a mirror.
While you come to know the other country, your own country is plain to see.
Experience abroad is one clip at a time.
Piled together, they shape me, as a participant.
